tldr: react-native-iap already advertises conformance to the OpenIAP spec, so the migration is mostly about adopting OpenIAP terminology and any newer spec types rather than swapping libraries. There is no separate OpenIAP runtime — it's a specification.
export_method: There is no third-party service to export from since OpenIAP is a spec. Audit your react-native-iap usage against the current OpenIAP spec at openiap.dev to identify divergences: type names, error code constants, and event semantics. List call sites that touch IAP types and group them by spec area (products, purchases, subscriptions, errors). Document any custom TypeScript wrappers or hooks built on top of the library — these are the most likely places where OpenIAP alignment changes naming or shape. Pull product IDs from store consoles for reference; they don't change. Capture your error handling matrix so you can re-map error codes to the OpenIAP names.
import_method: Read the OpenIAP spec on openiap.dev and check the react-native-iap version targets the spec version you want. Upgrade the library to a recent release if needed (the project uses Nitro Modules in current versions and ships frequent releases). Adjust your app code to use OpenIAP-aligned type names and error codes where the library exposes them. Refactor any custom wrappers, hooks, or state slices to mirror OpenIAP's product, purchase, and error shapes so future swaps to other OpenIAP-compliant libraries are trivial. There is no separate OpenIAP SDK to install. Keep server-side receipt validation untouched.
effort: 1-2 weeks for a typical RN app
gotchas: OpenIAP is a spec; you still need an implementing library — react-native-iap remains the runtime.
Spec is still maturing; breaking changes between versions are possible.
Library version must match the spec version you target — check the changelog.
Receipt validation is out of scope for OpenIAP; keep server-side checks intact.
Custom hooks and wrappers are the highest-friction refactor point.
Apple and Google may ship platform changes faster than the spec adapts.

email.subject: Aligning react-native-iap usage with the OpenIAP spec
email.body:
Hi team,

We plan to align our react-native-iap integration with the OpenIAP spec over the next 14 days. react-native-iap already targets OpenIAP, so this is mostly a naming and type-cleanup exercise rather than a library swap.

Proposed timeline:
Days 1-2: Audit current IAP code for divergences from OpenIAP — types, error codes, custom hooks and wrappers.
Days 3-5: Upgrade the library to a release that targets the spec version we want.
Days 6-9: Refactor wrappers and call sites to use OpenIAP-aligned names and shapes.
Days 10-12: Update tests and analytics to match the new type names; verify all platforms still work.
Days 13-14: QA pass on iOS and Android, regression-test purchases and restores, ship.

Server-side receipt validation stays as is. Please flag any custom IAP wrappers that may need redesign for spec alignment.

Thanks,
Your customer
